topic.name: Odd Man Out and Series
topic.description: Number series completion, error detection in sequences, and odd-one-out pattern recognition for aptitude exams. Estimated reading 25 min; practice 40 min.

theory.introduction: Odd Man Out and Series questions test your ability to detect underlying patterns in number sequences and identify the element that breaks the rule. Success depends on recognising common patterns — arithmetic and geometric progressions, squares, cubes, primes, alternating rules, and combined differences — and applying systematic elimination.
theory.explanation:
CONCEPT EXPLANATION

Number series questions fall into three main types:

1) Find the next (or missing) term: Analyse consecutive differences, ratios, or layered patterns until a consistent rule emerges.

2) Find the wrong number: Apply the detected pattern to each term; the one that violates the rule is the error.

3) Odd one out: Compare all terms against a single property (all primes, all squares, all multiples of 3, etc.) and pick the exception.

Common patterns:
• Arithmetic progression (AP): constant difference.
• Geometric progression (GP): constant ratio.
• Squares/cubes: 1, 4, 9, 16… or 1, 8, 27, 64…
• Combined differences: second-order differences are constant.
• Alternating series: two interleaved sub-series.
• Fibonacci-type: each term = sum of previous two.
• n² ± k, 2ⁿ − 1, n(n+1), and similar formula-based rules.

REAL-LIFE APPLICATIONS

Pattern recognition mirrors data trend analysis in business forecasting, quality-control anomaly detection, algorithm design, financial time-series analysis, and any field where spotting irregularities in sequential data is essential.

COMMON MISTAKES

1) Stopping at the first pattern that fits two or three terms without verifying the full series.
2) Ignoring alternating or interleaved sub-series.
3) Confusing "wrong number in series" with "odd one out" — wrong number breaks a sequential rule; odd one out breaks a category rule.
4) Overlooking second-order (difference of differences) patterns.
5) Assuming arithmetic progression when the series is geometric or polynomial.

IMPORTANT NOTES

• Always check differences first, then ratios, then squares/cubes.
• For mixed series, try splitting into odd-position and even-position sub-series.
• In odd-one-out, state the common property before picking the exception.
• Verify your answer fits every term, not just most of them.
• Typical exam series use integers; fractional or negative terms signal a different approach.
• When stuck, test simple patterns: +n, ×2, n², n²+n before exotic rules.

formulas.title: Arithmetic progression (nth term)
formulas.content: aₙ = a + (n − 1)d, where a = first term, d = common difference

formulas.title: Geometric progression (nth term)
formulas.content: aₙ = a × r^(n−1), where r = common ratio

formulas.title: Sum of first n natural numbers
Second-order difference
formulas.content: If first differences form an AP, the series follows a quadratic (n²-type) rule

shortcutTips.title: Difference first
shortcutTips.tip: Compute first differences; if not constant, compute second differences for polynomial patterns.

shortcutTips.title: Split alternating series
shortcutTips.tip: For 3, 5, 6, 10, 9, 15… check odd positions (3, 6, 9) and even positions (5, 10, 15) separately.

shortcutTips.title: Square and cube check
shortcutTips.tip: If terms grow fast, test whether each term is a perfect square or cube.

shortcutTips.title: Prime quick scan
shortcutTips.tip: In odd-one-out, check if all but one are prime, square, or divisible by a fixed number.

shortcutTips.title: Verify every term
shortcutTips.tip: For wrong-number questions, your rule must explain all terms except one.

shortcutTips.title: Ratio for GP
shortcutTips.tip: Divide consecutive terms; a constant ratio signals geometric progression.

solvedExamples.problem: Find the next term in the series: 2, 6, 12, 20, 30, ?
steps: First differences: 4, 6, 8, 10 — increasing by 2 each time.

solvedExamples.problem: Find the wrong number in the series: 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 62.
solvedExamples.solution: 62 is the wrong number; it should be 64.

steps: Check each number: 2, 3, 5, and 11 are prime numbers.
9 does not belong with the other prime numbers.

solvedExamples.problem: Find the next term in the series: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ?

exercise.title: General Questions
exercise.description: MCQ practice set on number series, wrong-number detection, and odd-one-out pattern questions.

questions.question: Find the next term in the series: 2, 6, 12, 20, 30, ?
questions.explanation: Differences are 4, 6, 8, 10; next difference is 12. 30 + 12 = 42.

questions.question: Find the next term in the series: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ?
questions.explanation: The series consists of perfect squares: 1², 2², 3², 4², 5². Next term = 6² = 36.

questions.question: Find the next term in the series: 5, 10, 20, 40, 80, ?
questions.explanation: Each term is multiplied by 2. Next term = 80 × 2 = 160.

questions.question: Find the next term in the series: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, ?
questions.explanation: The series lists consecutive prime numbers. The next prime after 11 is 13.

questions.question: Find the next term in the series: 3, 6, 11, 18, 27, ?
questions.explanation: Differences are +3, +5, +7, +9; next difference is +11. 27 + 11 = 38.

questions.question: Find the next term in the series: 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, ?
questions.explanation: Fibonacci series: each term is the sum of the two preceding terms. 5 + 8 = 13.

questions.question: Find the next term in the series: 121, 100, 81, 64, 49, ?
questions.explanation: Terms are descending perfect squares: 11², 10², 9², 8², 7². Next = 6² = 36.

questions.question: Find the next term in the series: 2, 6, 18, 54, ?
questions.explanation: Each term is multiplied by 3. Next term = 54 × 3 = 162.

questions.question: Find the next term in the series: 1, 3, 7, 15, 31, ?
questions.explanation: Pattern: 2ⁿ − 1. Next term = 2⁶ − 1 = 64 − 1 = 63.

questions.question: Find the next term in the series: 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, ?
questions.explanation: Each term is halved. Next term = 4 ÷ 2 = 2.

questions.question: Find the next term in the series: 7, 14, 28, 56, ?
questions.explanation: Geometric progression with ratio 2. Next term = 56 × 2 = 112.

questions.question: Find the next term in the series: 100, 95, 86, 73, 56, ?
questions.explanation: Differences: −5, −9, −13, −17; next difference is −21. 56 − 21 = 35.

questions.question: Find the wrong number in the series: 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 62.
questions.explanation: Each term doubles: 32 × 2 = 64, not 62. So 62 is the wrong number.

questions.question: Find the wrong number in the series: 3, 9, 27, 81, 243, 728.
questions.explanation: Powers of 3: 3¹, 3², 3³, 3⁴, 3⁵, 3⁶. 3⁶ = 729, not 728.

questions.question: Find the wrong number in the series: 5, 10, 15, 21, 25.
questions.explanation: The series increases by 5 each time. After 15 should come 20, not 21.

questions.question: Find the wrong number in the series: 1, 4, 9, 16, 24, 36.
questions.explanation: Perfect squares: 1², 2², 3², 4², 5², 6². 5² = 25, not 24.

questions.question: Find the wrong number in the series: 2, 6, 12, 20, 30, 43.
questions.explanation: Differences +4, +6, +8, +10 imply next difference +12. 30 + 12 = 42, not 43.

questions.question: Find the wrong number in the series: 7, 14, 21, 28, 36.
questions.explanation: Multiples of 7: 7, 14, 21, 28, 35. 36 is not a multiple of 7.

questions.question: Find the odd one out: 2, 3, 5, 9, 11.
questions.explanation: 2, 3, 5, and 11 are prime. 9 = 3 × 3 is composite.

questions.question: Find the odd one out: 4, 9, 16, 25, 27.
questions.explanation: 4, 9, 16, and 25 are perfect squares. 27 is not a perfect square.

questions.question: Find the odd one out: 11, 13, 17, 21, 23.
questions.explanation: 11, 13, 17, and 23 are prime. 21 = 3 × 7 is composite.

questions.question: Find the odd one out: 8, 27, 64, 100, 125.
questions.explanation: 8, 27, 64, and 125 are perfect cubes (2³, 3³, 4³, 5³). 100 is not a perfect cube.

questions.question: Find the odd one out: 3, 5, 7, 9, 11.
questions.explanation: 3, 5, 7, and 11 are prime numbers. 9 = 3 × 3 is not prime.

questions.question: Find the next term in the series: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, ?
questions.explanation: Arithmetic progression with common difference 4. Next term = 20 + 4 = 24.

questions.question: Find the next term in the series: 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, ?
questions.explanation: Fibonacci sequence: 5 + 8 = 13.
